skill.name: type-driven-design-rust
skill.description: Guides Rust developers in using type-driven design patterns such as newtypes, typestate, phantom types, and compile-time builders.
skill.summary: Design Rust APIs that encode invariants and prevent misuse at compile time.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, Rust teaching examples, ordinary words such as type-driven, and reference links. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, executable scripts, or secret access.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Rust fenced code blocks and closing fences in documentation. They do not contain shell commands, Ruby backticks, or executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows the reported locations are Markdown fences around Rust examples. No command execution syntax is present outside illustrative code snippets.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Misidentified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The URL findings are either example API strings inside Rust builder examples or normal documentation links in the resources section. No code performs network requests from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in Markdown text and Rust examples, not in executable scripts. No fetch, curl, wget, or equivalent runtime network operation was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings match words in the skill name, description, headings, and documentation links. The file does not implement cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines discuss type-driven design and Rust documentation. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Domain Modeling Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are ordinary Rust examples such as validation errors, IDs, and type modeling guidance. They do not inspect the host system or collect environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported lines are contained in static documentation examples. No filesystem enumeration, environment probing, or system information collection was found.

content.user_title: Design Safer Rust APIs With Types
content.value_statement: Rust APIs can hide invalid states until runtime. This skill helps Codex, Claude, and Claude Code encode invariants in types.
actual_capabilities: Explains newtype, typestate, phantom type, builder, and session type patterns.
Shows before-and-after Rust examples for common API misuse problems.
Helps model domain invariants as explicit Rust types.
Guides compile-time enforcement of state transitions and required builder fields.
Explains trade-offs between type safety, ergonomics, and compile-time complexity.
Provides checklist-style guidance for applying type-driven design to existing APIs.
limitations: It is a Markdown guidance skill, not an executable Rust library.
Examples may need adaptation for each project, crate version, and error model.
It does not verify code by running cargo check or tests automatically.
It focuses on Rust API design and does not cover every advanced type-level technique.

use_cases.title: Model domain identifiers safely
use_cases.description: Convert raw strings, numbers, and UUIDs into newtypes that prevent accidental value mixups.

use_cases.title: Enforce workflow states
use_cases.description: Design typestate APIs for connections, transactions, reviews, or other stateful processes.

use_cases.title: Improve builders for required fields
use_cases.description: Replace runtime builder validation with compile-time guarantees for mandatory configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Find useful newtypes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a Rust domain model
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust module and suggest where newtypes would prevent mixing IDs, units, or validated values.

prompt_templates.title: Design a typestate flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me model this workflow as a Rust typestate API. Identify states, valid transitions, and invalid calls that should not compile.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a builder
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Rust builder so required fields are enforced at compile time while optional fields stay ergonomic.

prompt_templates.title: Audit type-driven trade-offs
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this proposed type-driven Rust API. Explain safety benefits, ergonomics risks, compile-time costs, and a simpler alternative if needed.

output_examples.input: A payment API accepts raw account IDs and amounts as plain values.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend separate account and amount types, validated constructors, and function signatures that prevent swapped arguments.

output_examples.input: A connection object panics if methods are called before authentication.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline disconnected, connected, and authenticated states with transitions that make invalid calls unavailable.

output_examples.input: A configuration builder returns runtime errors for missing host and port fields.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose typed builder states so construction only finishes after required fields are supplied.

best_practices: Start with important domain invariants before adding type-level complexity.
Keep public APIs ergonomic with clear constructors, conversions, and error messages.
Use compile-fail tests or examples to document invalid states that should not compile.
anti_patterns: Do not use typestate when states are highly dynamic and data-driven.
Do not expose complex marker types when a simple validated newtype is enough.
Do not hide validation failures behind panics when constructors can return errors.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete Rust crates?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance and examples that you adapt inside your Rust project.

faq.question: Can it help with existing Rust code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review existing APIs and suggest newtypes, typestate transitions, or typed builders.

faq.question: Does type-driven design add runtime overhead?
faq.answer: Often no. Newtypes and phantom markers usually compile away, but each design should be checked in context.

faq.question: When should I avoid typestate?
faq.answer: Avoid it when state transitions are too dynamic or when the API becomes harder to use than the runtime checks it replaces.

faq.question: Does it replace Rust tests?
faq.answer: No. It reduces some invalid states, but tests still cover behavior, integration, and edge cases.

faq.question: Is it useful for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is written for AI coding assistants that explain, review, and refactor Rust APIs.
